document_text:
Evaluating randomized smoothing as a defense
against adversarial attacks in trajectory prediction
Julian F. Schumann∗, Eduardo Figueiredo†, Frederik Baymler Mathiesen†,
Luca Laurenti†, Jens Kober∗, Arkady Zgonnikov∗
∗Department of Cognitive Robotics,TU Delft, Delft, Netherlands
†Delft Center for Systems and Control,TU Delft, Delft, Netherlands
{j.f.schumannn, e.figueiredo, f.b.mathiesen, l.laurenti, j.kober, a.zgonnikov}@tudelft.nl
Abstract—Accurate and robust trajectory prediction is es-
sential for safe and efficient autonomous driving, yet recent
work has shown that even state-of-the-art prediction models
are highly vulnerable to inputs being mildly perturbed by
adversarial attacks. Although model vulnerabilities to such
attacks have been studied, work on effective countermeasures
remains limited. In this work, we develop and evaluate a
new defense mechanism for trajectory prediction models based
on randomized smoothing – an approach previously applied
successfully in other domains. We evaluate its ability to improve
model robustness through a series of experiments that test
different strategies of randomized smoothing. We show that
our approach can consistently improve prediction robustness of
multiple base trajectory prediction models in various datasets
without compromising accuracy in non-adversarial settings. Our
results demonstrate that randomized smoothing offers a sim-
ple and computationally inexpensive technique for mitigating
adversarial attacks in trajectory prediction.
Index Terms—autonomous vehicles, trajectory prediction,
adversarial attack, randomized smoothing.
I. INTRODUCTION
Autonomous driving has emerged as a key automotive
innovation, attracting billions in investment [1], driven by
its potential to reduce traffic injuries and fatalities [2] as
well as to generate substantial economic value [3]. However,
for the viability of autonomous vehicles, being safer than
human drivers alone is insufficient; they must also drive
efficiently and interact smoothly with human road users.
Early autonomous vehicles were often overly cautious, which
paradoxically created risks, such as being rear-ended [4],
[5]. This arises from the uncertainty about other road users’
behavior, limiting safe control options. Accurate prediction
models can reduce this uncertainty, enabling more efficient
and assertive driving, anticipating conflicts, and yielding
when appropriate [6], [7], while also supporting realistic
simulation of human behavior in virtual environments [8].
Consequently, many distinct prediction models have been
proposed in recent years, with a primary focus on deep
learning methods. The employed architectures range from
conditional variational autoencoders [9], [10] and normaliz-
The source code used in this work is available online at
https://github.com/julianschumann/General-Framework-Smoothing.ing flows [11] to denoising diffusion models [12], [13] and
large language models [14], [15].
While these models generally achieve impressive predic-
tion accuracy on well-defined benchmarks, their robustness
– although essential for public safety [16] – is explored
far less thoroughly. One emerging method for evaluating
robustness is based on adversarial trajectories, which are
specifically generated to deceive the model’s predictions as
much as possible; a model’s ability to withstand such attacks
is known as adversarial robustness [17]. Initial work on
adversarial attack generation has shown promise in exposing
system vulnerabilities [18]–[21]. However, these works typ-
ically compare the model predictions under adversarial per-
turbations against ground-truth future trajectories of benign
agents, which might not be kinematically reachable from the
perturbed past on which these predictions are conditioned.
Recent work has introduced additional constraints ensuring
kinematic reachability to make such comparisons more physi-
cally meaningful [22]. However, even under those more con-
strained and meaningful attacks, though, most state-of-the-
art models still show significant degradation in performance.
Additionally, such adversarial attacks have been shown to
be remarkably transferable between different models when
trained on similar datasets [22], [23], further highlighting the
importance of trajectory prediction robustness for real-world
automated driving systems.
While prior work has examined the design and evaluation
of adversarial attacks [16], research on countering such
attacks in trajectory prediction models is very limited. Re-
cently, Fanet al.proposed a method for detecting adversarial
trajectories [24] which could enable an autonomous vehicle
to avoid being misled by an adversarial agent. However, be-
yond this work, systematic approaches to defending trajectory
prediction models against adversarial attacks remain largely
unexplored. At the same time, outside trajectory prediction,
randomized smoothinghas proven to be an effective coun-
termeasure against adversarial attacks [25]. This technique
(introduced in details in Section II-B), perturbs the input data
with random noise and returns an averaged prediction, thus
attenuating the effect of any specific adversarial modification.
The approach has been successfully applied in computerarXiv:2603.10821v1  [cs.LG]  11 Mar 2026

noisy pastFig. 1. An overview of randomized smoothing applied to trajectory prediction. a) In a benign scenario, it can be expected that a well-trained trajectory
prediction modelPcan make predictions which align with the ground truth. b) However, if the target agent, for which predictions are made, uses an
adversarial attack, the prediction quality can deteriorate even in state-of-the-art models [22], [23]. c) Randomized smoothing can be used to overcome this
issue. Instead of the actual past observations of the adversarial agent, the model is applied to multiple randomly perturbed versions of these observations.
The final prediction is then built by averaging those separate model predictions.
vision [26], [27] and language models [28], [29]. However,
to the best of our knowledge, it has not yet been applied to
trajectory prediction.
In this work, we propose a methodology for applying ran-
domized smoothing to arbitrary trajectory prediction models
(as illustrated in Figure 1) and assess its efficacy against
realistic adversarial attacks. After introducing the concept of
adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction and therandom-
ized smoothingtechnique (Sections II-A and II-B, respec-
tively), we present two strategies for smoothing trajectory
predictors in Section III. Then, we empirically evaluate our
proposed methods in Section IV. In particular, we gener-
ate adversarial attacks on two datasets (L-GAP[30] and
rounD[31]) following [22], and evaluate the impact of
the randomized smoothing (implemented following [32]) on
established trajectory prediction models (Trajectron++[9]
andADAPT[33]) using select robustness metrics.
II. BACKGROUND
A. Adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction models
In trajectory prediction, the goal is to forecast the fu-
ture movementY i={s i(t)|t∈ {∆t, . . . , T∆t}}, where
s∈RDis recorded in intervals of∆t, of an agent

imize the distance between bYiand ground truth observation
Yi, or to maximize the likelihoodP θ(Yi|Xi,X¬i)[22]1.
An attack by an adversarial target agent “tar” (with index
i=tar) then attempts to deceive the prediction model
employed by the autonomous vehicle into making incorrect
predictions. Specifically, the observed (past) states of the
target agent are modified using perturbationsδ X, resulting
in the perturbed past trajectory eXtar=X tar+δXand the
corresponding predictionsbeYtar∼P θ(·|eXtar,X¬tar), which
can then used to evaluate the robustness of the models.
While there have been many works on adversarial attacks
on trajectory prediction models [18]–[21] suggesting different
methods for generatingδ X, in this work we will use the
kinematically constrained attacks proposed by Schumannet
al.[22]. This method was chosen because it is the only avail-

B. Randomized smoothing
Randomized Smoothing[25], [35], [36] has recently
emerged as one of the primary techniques for improving the
robustness of machine learning models. While the literature
has initially focused on classification problems [25], [27],
[37], [38], analogous results have been then presented for the
regression setting [32], [39], [40], which we briefly introduce
in this Section. Letf:Rd→Rqbe abaseregression model.
Therandomized smoothedregression model is defined as
g(x) =E ε
f(x+ε)
,(2)
whereε∼ N(0, σ2I)is (typically) a Gaussian random noise
with varianceσ2>0. Intuitively, Equation (2) represents a
regressor averaging the value of the base regressorfin a
neighborhood around the inputx∈Rd. In practice, comput-
ing Equation (2) in closed-form is often infeasible [41] (e.g.
due to the potential non-linearity off, which is the case, for
instance, iffis a neural network). Thus, this expression is
generally replaced by the natural approximation
gN(x) =1
NNX
i=1f(x+ε i),(3)
where{ε 1, . . . , ε N}are i.i.d. samples from the distribution
ofε. The main advantage of smoothed regressors is that they
inherit some particular robustness properties against attacks
inL p-norm, without any need to retrain the base regressor
[32], [40]. Indeed, Theorem 3 in [32] shows that, for a
given inputx∈Rd, one may define a compact setRthat
gN(x+δ)belongs to with high probability for any sufficiently
small attackδ. In other words, by averaging predictions
in a neighborhood of the pointx, the smoothed regressor
becomes less sensitive to inputs associated with high-norm
gradients, thus regularizing the prediction output. The insight
that averaging mechanisms can protect against adversarial
attacks has also been observed in the context of Bayesian
models [42].III. RANDOMIZED SMOOTHING FOR TRAJECTORY
PREDICTION
After giving a short overview over both adversarial attacks
against trajectory prediction models and randomized smooth-
ing, we apply the latter to trajectory prediction. Specifically,
we first assume that for each agenti, the sampling of
Kindependent trajectories from the prediction distribution
Pθ(.|X i,X¬i)described in Section II-A induces a base
(stochastic) prediction function2fPθ:RH×D→RK×T×D
such that
bYi=fPθ(Xi).(4)
Furthermore, we assume that a trajectory states=
{p,v,u} ∈RDcan be partitioned into a positionp, some
auxiliary statesv, and the control inputsu, where the
latter are distinguished by being associated with the highest
order of derivatives ofpover time. Based on this, we then
propose two approaches of randomized smoothing for the
base prediction modelf Pθpresented in Equation (4).
A. Position-based smoothing
Our first approach consists in smoothing the position co-
ordinates of the vehicles’ states, thus obtaining the smoothed
predictorg Ngiven by:
gN(X) =1

whereΣ Xis only non-zero in the block-diagonal associated
with the positionsp, to which we assign the varianceσ2>0.
B. Control-based smoothing
Alternatively, under the assumption that there exists a
dynamical modelφsuch that{p(t),v(t)}=φ(s(t−∆t)),
we define the functionΦwithX tar= Φ(U tar), whereU tar

We then propose a second smoothing approach, where the
noise is applied to the control inputs, similarly to how the
perturbations are applied to them in [22]:
gN(X) =1

This approach – which could be described as a (higher-order)
Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process [43] – is theorized to have the
advantage that the resulting trajectories are less likely to
display dynamically infeasible inputs (such as impossible
sharp turns), and are therefore less prone to degrade model
performance, if the model would rely on such properties.
The covarianceΣ Uis only non-zero on the diagonal entries
associated with the control inputsu. Here, to allow for
a fair comparison with theposition-basedapproach, the
variance levels are chosen so that the noise accumulated

IV. EVALUATION
A. Experiment setup
To empirically evaluate the potential of randomized
smoothing in the context of adversarial attacks against tra-
jectory prediction models, we test the approaches introduced
in Section III (position-based in Equation (5) – henceforth
referred aspos– and control-based in Equation (7) –
ctrl) on two datasets and two base prediction models. For
the datasets, following [22], we considerL-GAP[30] and
rounD[31]. InL-GAP[30] (a driving simulator dataset), a
vehicle performing a left turn at an intersection is assumed
to be adversarial (similar to Figure 1); it performs a turn
across the path of an oncoming vehicle (the ego agent).
InrounD[31] (a real-world dataset collected on German
roundabouts), the ego agent is on the roundabout and the
adversarial agent tries to enter the roundabout in front of
the ego agent. For the base trajectory prediction models, we
employTrajectron++[9] andADAPT[33], two state-of-the-
art models which are trained on each of the datasets. To
compensate forL-GAP’s small size, the models evaluated
on that dataset are trained on a combination ofL-GAPand
NuScenes[44].
The loss functionLused for generating adversarial attacks
(Equation 1) is defined as the average displacement error
(ADE) of the prediction model. Perturbations are applied not
to the trajectories themselves but to the underlying control
inputs, subject to further constraints that ensure that the attack
remains meaningful (see Schumannet al.[22] for a more
detailed description). This approach was selected because it
produces adversarial attacks that are kinematically feasible
and not easily detectable by simple filters.
In each experiment, we run multiple attacks by varying the
perturbation limitd max∈ {0.25 m,0.5 m,1 m}. The ADE
metric is then also used to assess the performance of ran-
domized smoothing, which is performed with various levels
of noise standard deviation, i.e.σ∈ {0.25 m,0.5 m,1 m}.
Further, we empirically evaluate the impact of the training
strategy, i.e. either employing smoothing during the training
phase of the base predictor3–train&eval, or the standard
practice of smoothing only during evaluation –eval. In all
experiments, evaluation is performed withN= 20.
B. Results and discussion
The results in Tables I, II, III, and IV suggest that, com-
pared with the base prediction models, employing random-
ized smoothing improves models’ ADE performance in all

adversarial attack experiments (i.e.,d max>0). Additionally,
with the exception ofTrajectron++onrounD(Table III),
randomized smoothing (either position-based or control-
based) achieved comparable performance on the unperturbed
datasets as well (i.e,d max= 0), and in one case (ADAPT
onrounD; Table IV) even improved baseline performance.
While earlier work in other domains demonstrated that
randomized smoothing typically deteriorates performance in
nominal scenarios [25], [36], our results support the idea that
randomized smoothing can function as a form of regulariza-
tion that protects against model overfitting [45].
Furthermore, we observe that randomized smoothing is
capable of lowering the correlation between the perturbation
limitd maxand the resulting ADE. For instance, when apply-
ing the control-based approach (ctrl, Equation (7)) with noise
variance levelσ= 0.25 mto the base modelADAPT, the
ADE differences betweend max= 0.25 mandd max= 1 m
are much less severe (−0.81 mvs.1.095 monL-GAPin
Table II and0.028 mvs.0.92 monrounDin Table IV), which
suggests a reduction of the impact of the attack magnitude
on the model predictions in general.
The effect of the training strategy is, however, less clear.
For instance, employing randomized smoothing during the
training phase (train&eval) improves the results forTra-
jectron++for both the position- (pos) and control-based
(ctrl) approaches (Table III), while forADAPT, it is only
beneficial for the position-based. Similarly, the impact of the
smoothing approaches in Equations (5) and (7) is model-

dependent4. Concretely,ADAPT’s best results are generally
achieved when smoothing control inputs (Tables II and IV).
In contrast, forTrajectron++, if smoothing is only used for
evaluation, the position-based approachpos, with very few
exceptions, presents superior results. Given the lack of clear
preferences, future work might also try to explore the benefit
of combining both approaches.
Meanwhile, the effect of noise varianceσ2is much more
pronounced, and the smallest ADEs are achieved most often
with the smallest values ofσ(the noticeable exceptions are
ford max= 0.25 min Table II andd max= 1 min Table III).
This seems reasonable, as lower variances likely allow for a
better balance between increasing the prediction robustness
while maintaining the local accuracy of the base model.
4While we tried to make the impact of the similar values ofσacross the
two approaches comparable, the validity of such a comparison cannot be
guaranteed.Importantly, given that randomized smoothing is an ap-
proach that promises increased robustness without retraining
the base model, it is remarkable that, for bothTrajectron++
andADAPT, we can find at least one smoothing setting which
consistently improves on the base model across all datasets
ford max>0. In particular, forTrajectron++, this is the case
for the position-based (pos) approach whenσ= 0.25 m,
while forADAPT, we can point to the control-based (ctrl)
approach also withσ= 0.25 m.
While our results suggest that randomized smoothing is a
promising technique for enhancing the robustness of trajec-
tory prediction models, future work should explore whether
the probabilistic robustness guarantees in [32] generate tight
useful prediction sets in the case of trajectory prediction,
while also further exploring theirα-smoothing technique.
Furthermore, it is interesting to explore whether the variance
of the randomized predictor (i.e.,f(x+ε)) could be used
as a reliable proxy for the uncertainty of the base predictor,
which would be useful information for the motion planner
using such predictions. Finally, expanding to more models,
datasets, and adversarial attack methods would improve the
support for the claims in this section.
V. CONCLUSION
In this work, we explored randomized smoothing as a
defense mechanism for trajectory prediction models against
adversarial attacks. Our results indicate that this technique
consistently improves performance (i.e., reduced average
displacement error) of trajectory predictions under adver-
sarial attacks, while also lowering the model sensitivity to
the magnitude of the adversarial perturbation. Interestingly,
we observe that the smoothed predictors exhibit at most
a marginal reduction in accuracy on the original, non-
adversarial data, even achieving improved performance in
some settings. Consequently, randomized smoothing seems
to be capable of consistently improving empirical robustness
for the base models across datasets and perturbation magni-
tudes studied in this work. Even though our results indicate
that the ideal smoothing approach is model-dependent, our
work positions randomized smoothing as a computationally
inexpensive and practical tool for increasing the robustness
of trajectory prediction systems, and motivate future work on
certified robustness guarantees in this domain.

structure.title: INTRODUCTION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the vulnerability of trajectory prediction models to adversarial attacks in autonomous driving. It highlights the importance of robustness in trajectory prediction for safe and efficient driving. The document proposes a defense mechanism based on randomized smoothing, which involves perturbing input data with random noise and averaging predictions to mitigate adversarial attacks. The approach is evaluated through experiments on various datasets and trajectory prediction models, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving model robustness without compromising accuracy. The main points covered include the introduction of adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction, the concept of randomized smoothing, and the evaluation of the proposed methodology against realistic adversarial attacks.

structure.title: BACKGROUND
structure.summary: The partial document discusses trajectory prediction in various scenarios, including benign and adversarial attacks. It introduces the concept of randomized smoothing as a technique to improve the robustness of trajectory prediction models against adversarial attacks. The document outlines the methodology for applying randomized smoothing to arbitrary trajectory prediction models and evaluates its efficacy against realistic adversarial attacks on two datasets. The main points covered include the definition of trajectory prediction, the concept of adversarial attacks, and the application of randomized smoothing to improve model robustness. The document also touches on the background of trajectory prediction, including the goal of forecasting future movement and the training of probabilistic models to generate trajectory samples.

nodes.title: Adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction models
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses trajectory prediction in various scenarios, including benign and adversarial attacks. It introduces the concept of randomized smoothing as a technique to improve the robustness of machine learning models against adversarial attacks. The document proposes two strategies for applying randomized smoothing to trajectory prediction models: position-based smoothing and control-based smoothing. The position-based approach involves smoothing the position coordinates of the vehicles' states, while the control-based approach applies noise to the control inputs. The document also provides background information on adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction models and the concept of randomized smoothing. The main points covered include the application of randomized smoothing to trajectory prediction, the proposal of two smoothing approaches, and the discussion of adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction models.

nodes.title: Randomized smoothing
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses two main topics: adversarial attacks and randomized smoothing for trajectory prediction models. The first part describes a "white-box" attack approach that generates realistic attacks while preserving the original behavior of the target vehicle, using projected gradient descent to produce an attack starting from the unperturbed input. The second part introduces randomized smoothing, a technique for improving the robustness of machine learning models, which has been applied to classification and regression problems. The document then applies randomized smoothing to trajectory prediction, proposing two approaches: position-based smoothing and control-based smoothing. The position-based smoothing involves adding noise to the position coordinates of the vehicles' states, while the control-based smoothing involves adding noise to the control inputs. The goal of these approaches is to improve the robustness of trajectory prediction models against adversarial attacks.

structure.title: SMOOTHING TRAJECTORY PREDICTORS
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the application of randomized smoothing to improve the robustness of trajectory prediction models against adversarial attacks. The main points covered include the introduction of a "white-box" attack using projected gradient descent, the concept of randomized smoothing, and its application to trajectory prediction models. The document also presents two approaches to randomized smoothing: position-based smoothing and control-based smoothing. The results of experiments on two datasets and two base prediction models show that randomized smoothing improves the models' performance in terms of average displacement error (ADE) and reduces the impact of adversarial attacks. The document also discusses the effect of the training strategy and the comparison between the two smoothing approaches. Overall, the document explores the potential of randomized smoothing as a regularization technique to protect against model overfitting and improve the robustness of trajectory prediction models.

structure.title: EVALUATION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of randomized smoothing in the context of adversarial attacks against trajectory prediction models. The main points covered include the experiment setup, where two datasets (L-GAP and rounD) and two base prediction models (Trajectron++ and ADAPT) are used to test the approaches. The results show that randomized smoothing improves the models' performance in terms of average displacement error (ADE) in all adversarial attack experiments. The document also discusses the impact of the training strategy and the smoothing approaches on the results, suggesting that randomized smoothing can function as a form of regularization that protects against model overfitting. Additionally, the results indicate that randomized smoothing can reduce the correlation between the perturbation limit and the resulting ADE, making the model predictions more robust to adversarial attacks.

nodes.title: Experiment setup
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of randomized smoothing in the context of adversarial attacks against trajectory prediction models. The main points covered include the experiment setup, where two datasets (L-GAP and rounD) and two base prediction models (Trajectron++ and ADAPT) are used to test the approaches. The results show that randomized smoothing improves the models' performance in all adversarial attack experiments, and in some cases, even improves the baseline performance on unperturbed datasets. The document also discusses the impact of the training strategy and the smoothing approaches on the results, highlighting the potential of randomized smoothing as a form of regularization that protects against model overfitting.

nodes.title: Results and discussion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the application of randomized smoothing as a defense mechanism for trajectory prediction models against adversarial attacks. The main points covered include the experimental setup, where two datasets (L-GAP and rounD) and two base prediction models (Trajectron++ and ADAPT) are used to evaluate the potential of randomized smoothing. The results show that randomized smoothing improves the models' average displacement error (ADE) performance in all adversarial attack experiments, while also lowering the model sensitivity to the magnitude of the adversarial perturbation. The document also explores the impact of different smoothing approaches, noise variance levels, and training strategies on the performance of the models. Overall, the results suggest that randomized smoothing is a promising technique for enhancing the robustness of trajectory prediction models, and future work should explore its potential in more models, datasets, and adversarial attack methods.

structure.title: CONCLUSION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the application of randomized smoothing as a defense mechanism for trajectory prediction models against adversarial attacks. The main points covered include the presentation of results from experiments using two models, Trajectron++ and ADAPT, with different smoothing approaches and noise variances. The results show that randomized smoothing can improve the robustness of trajectory prediction models, with the best results often achieved when smoothing control inputs. The document also highlights the importance of exploring the benefits of combining different smoothing approaches and the need for future work on certified robustness guarantees. Additionally, the references provided suggest a comprehensive review of existing literature on trajectory prediction, adversarial attacks, and randomized smoothing. Overall, the partial document focuses on the empirical evaluation of randomized smoothing for trajectory prediction models and its potential to enhance their robustness against adversarial attacks.
